_meta.note: delta_v2 format — Mar 2026 FY-end confirmation issue. All signals are FY-end confirmed across Jan 2024–Mar 2026 merged series.

editorial.context_strip: Every year the RBI publishes a March year-end SIBC — the full fiscal year picture in one file. India Credit Lens now has three years of data merged into a single series. Issue #3 is the FY-end confirmation: every signal we tracked this year now has a final number.
editorial.hero_narrative: ₹213.6L Cr. +17.1% YoY. FY26 closes as the strongest credit year since FY22 — and every major signal we tracked came in stronger than the interim reads suggested. MSME growth tripled from FY25 to FY26. Gold loans confirmed a 5× move over two years. The NBFC channel swung from +7.4% to +26.3% in a single year. And for the first time in this dataset, all four main sectors — industry, services, personal loans, and agriculture — accelerated simultaneously. The FY-end file doesn't introduce new stories. It closes the ones we've been tracking and sets the stakes for FY27.
tldr: ₹213.6L Cr total bank credit. +17.1% YoY. FY26 is the strongest credit expansion year since FY22.
MSME confirmed at +33.1% YoY — 4× the FY25 growth rate. PSL Micro & Small independently corroborates at +29.5%. Two series, same direction, both confirmed at FY-end.
Gold loans: ₹0.93L Cr → ₹4.60L Cr in two years. The reclassification stock effect is fully recognised. FY27 growth will be flow-only — real disbursements, no more one-time tailwinds.

All four main sectors accelerated simultaneously for the first time in this dataset: Industry +15.0%, Services +19.0%, Personal Loans +16.2%, Agriculture +15.7%. The 2023-24 policy tightening cycle is fully absorbed. FY27 will test whether this is a structural expansion or a catch-up cycle.
editorial.system_narrative: Three signals we tracked across Issues #1 and #2 now have their FY-end numbers, and all three came in stronger. MSME went from an interim +31% to a confirmed +33.1% at year-end — the 4× acceleration over FY25 is not a base-period artefact. Gold loans closed at ₹4.60L Cr, a 5× move from March 2024, but the March 2026 file also confirms the reclassification stock effect is complete — FY27 gold loan growth will be a clean read on real demand. The NBFC channel reopened sharply: +26.3% FY-end after +7.4% in FY25, nearly 4× the FY25 incremental in a single year. What makes this FY-end file distinctively different from the interim reads is the synchronisation signal: every main sector — industry, services, personal loans, agriculture — accelerated in FY26. That has not happened once in the two prior years of this dataset. Whether that synchronisation holds into FY27 is the only question that matters now.

signals.signal: All four main sectors accelerated simultaneously — a first in this dataset
signals.stat: +17.1% YoY system — Industry +15.0%, Services +19.0%, Personal Loans +16.2%, Agriculture +15.7%
signals.body: The March 2026 FY-end file closes FY26 at ₹213.6L Cr (+17.1% YoY) — the strongest credit year since FY22. But the more significant signal is the synchronisation: all four main sectors accelerated in FY26. In FY24 and FY25, personal loans ran far ahead while industry lagged. In FY26, industry reaccelerated to +15.0%, services surged to +19.0% (its highest in the dataset), agriculture moved to +15.7% — all while personal loans held at +16.2%. This degree of broad-based acceleration has not appeared once in the Jan 2024–Mar 2025 data. It either marks a genuine structural expansion — formalisation, PLI capex, NBFC channel reopening all converging — or the leading edge of a credit cycle that will produce stress in FY27-28. The FY-end confirmation doesn't resolve that question; it makes it urgent.
signals.implication: Any credit portfolio that underperformed 16% system growth in FY26 has lost market share during the system's strongest expansion year. The FY27 question: does synchronised acceleration reflect structural demand, or will first-cycle borrower stress and NBFC re-tightening compress the growth rate?

signals.signal: MSME credit growth tripled from FY25 to FY26 — confirmed by two independent series
signals.stat: Micro & Small +33.1% YoY (₹10.63L Cr) | PSL MSE +29.5% FY — both confirmed at FY-end
signals.body: FY26 closes with Micro & Small credit at +33.1% YoY — against +9.6% in FY25. The FY26 incremental alone is ₹2.65L Cr, four times the ₹0.65L Cr FY25 add. The PSL Micro & Small series confirms independently at +29.5% FY. Two measurement approaches, both confirmed at year-end, both pointing to the same structural acceleration. The driver: GST-registered MSMEs from 2019-22 now have 4-6 years of digital financial history — enough for alt-data underwriting models to run. A meaningful share of this growth is first-cycle formal borrowers with thin or blank bureau histories. Bureau-only underwriting cannot scale to this book. FY27-28 will be the first stress test for alt-data models as the first-cycle borrower loss curves begin to emerge.
signals.implication: The MSME formalisation wave is confirmed at scale. The window for alt-data underwriting model training is now — GST + UPI + e-way-bill data is 4+ years deep. FY27 growth will be the first real test: can disbursement velocity hold above 20% when the base is now ₹10.63L Cr instead of ₹8.08L Cr?

signals.signal: Bank credit to NBFCs nearly 4× the FY25 incremental in a single year
signals.stat: ₹20.66L Cr (+26.3% FY-end) — FY26 incremental ₹4.30L Cr vs ₹1.12L Cr in FY25
signals.body: Bank credit to NBFCs closed FY26 at ₹20.66L Cr, up from ₹16.35L Cr in Mar 2025 — a 26.3% FY move and nearly 4× the FY25 incremental. The 2023 risk-weight tightening cycle that compressed NBFC funding through FY25 is fully absorbed. The reopening is confirmed at FY-end, not just in interim months. The bank-NBFC relationship is now the fastest-growing major services sub-category, overtaking the prior year's infrastructure-led services composition. The infrastructure wedge: banks need NBFC origination reach in MSME, gold, and vehicle lending; NBFCs need bank balance sheet capacity. Co-origination agreements, warehouse lines, and loan-management systems with co-lending support are the product wedge.
signals.implication: The NBFC funding channel is fully open. Fintechs and NBFCs with bank co-lending agreements are now running on the system's fastest-growing funding source. The risk: RBI could re-tighten risk weights if it perceives NBFC overheating — watch H1 FY27 FSR.

signals._comment: Prior signals auto-rendered from signal_registry.json — no confirmed entries needed here

what_to_watch.next_release: May/June 2026 (RBI SIBC Jan 2027 — first FY27 interim read)
bullets: Gold loan FY27 disbursements — flow-only growth after two years of stock effects. Can the underlying demand sustain above 30%? Gold price reversal >20% is the primary risk — LTV ratios on marginal loans are already aggressive.

MSME first-cycle borrower loss curves — FY26 onboarded a wave of first-cycle formal borrowers with thin bureau histories. FY27 is the first vintage year where delinquency data will emerge. Alt-data underwriting models face their first real test.
NBFC risk-weight re-tightening — the FY26 reopening was sharp (+26.3%). RBI monitors NBFC systemic risk closely. Watch H1 FY27 Financial Stability Report for any re-tightening signal.
Renewable energy project finance drawdowns — the FY27-28 infrastructure credit wave. Watch for any Infrastructure sub-sector crossing +15% YoY in the next SIBC as PPA-to-drawdown timelines mature.
